codeReview.summary: The implementation cleanly integrates GenericAcp into the iOS agent picker across 4 files. It adds ACP profile selection in the picker sheet, a custom command text field with shell character validation, ACP badge pills on ACP-type agent rows, persistence via AgentSelectionStore, and wiring of remoteCommand through the SSH connect flow. The code is well-structured, follows project conventions (4-space indent, dark theme, litterFont usage), and includes 9 new unit tests covering the persistence layer. There are two minor non-blocking issues: duplicated shell validation logic and a subtle UX edge case in handleGenericAcpSelection.

issues.description: containsDangerousShellCharacters is duplicated verbatim from ACPProfilesView.swift (line 359). This identical private function exists in two views with the same logic and same dangerous character list. Should be extracted to a shared utility (e.g., an extension on String or a ShellValidation helper) to avoid drift. If one copy is updated, the other may be missed.

handleGenericAcpSelection() only auto-selects when there's exactly 1 profile. When there are 0 profiles, clicking a detected GenericAcp agent from agentInfos does nothing — the user clicks and nothing visible happens (the function body is a no-op for 0 profiles). The acpProfilesSection below does show 'No ACP profiles configured' with a dismiss-and-go-to-Settings hint, but tapping the detected GenericAcp row itself gives no feedback. Consider either disabling the detected GenericAcp row when no profiles exist, or showing an alert/feedback when tapped with 0 profiles.

hasRemoteCommand is passed as a string 'true'/'false' in the metadata dictionary. This is fine for logging/diagnostics, but worth noting that the actual command value is passed separately via the Rust FFI call. The metadata dict entry is only used for tracing, so this is a style note, not a bug.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Shell character validation logic (containsDangerousShellCharacters) is duplicated across ACPProfilesView.swift and AgentPickerView.swift with identical implementations. AGENTS.md mentions 'provider-agnostic naming' and keeping bridge files thin, but doesn't mention validation utility deduplication patterns. This is a common enough pattern that a shared utility convention would help.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AgentPickerView.swift:725-729 and ACPProfilesView.swift:359-362 — identical private functions with same dangerous character list

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature uses UserDefaults for persistence of ACP profile selection and remote command via AgentSelectionStore. Other stores in the project (ACPProfileStore, SavedServerStore) use Codable+JSON in UserDefaults as well. AGENTS.md mentions 'Codable migrations must decode BOTH old and new keys' but doesn't establish a convention for when to use UserDefaults vs other persistence. The AgentSelectionStore uses raw string/UUID keys rather than Codable, which is a slightly different pattern.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AgentPickerView.swift:200-240 — new persistence methods use string/UUID UserDefaults keys directly

sharedStateObservations.observation: The platform-ui-worker skill was not found at .factory/skills/platform-ui-worker/SKILL.md. The feature's skillName is 'platform-ui-worker' but the skill file doesn't exist in the expected location. The worker's skillFeedback says followedProcedure=true, so the skill may exist at a different path or may have been loaded from a different mechanism.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Glob for .factory/**/platform-ui-worker/** returned no results, yet features.json references skillName 'platform-ui-worker'

summary: The ios-agent-picker-acp feature is well-implemented and meets all expected behaviors: ACP profiles appear in the agent picker, custom command field with validation is provided, ACP badge pills distinguish ACP agents, and selection/command persistence works via UserDefaults. The 9 new tests adequately cover the persistence layer. Two non-blocking issues were identified: duplicated shell validation logic that should be extracted to a shared utility, and a minor UX edge case where tapping a detected GenericAcp row with 0 profiles gives no user feedback. Overall the code follows project conventions, is well-structured, and passes all tests (106 iOS, 1250 Rust).
